figures.caption: Table 11: Ablation results (base: N = 4, T = 3, S = 1, K = 2, d = 4096, lr= 1.5×10−4, warmup= 1000, 10k steps). ∆ relative to respective base. The base HRM NT=12 val CE (4.177) differs from EqualParam (4.284) due to the lower LR used here; both are the same architecture and data.
Table 24: UniTF d = 7296 val CE trajectory (1218M, 10k complete).
Table 10: Latency — inference metrics (seq=1024, bfloat16, RTX 6000, no KV cache). †: d = 2048, others d = 4096. Ratio vs. T-L4.

Figure 4: EqualFLOPs learning curves (10k steps, corrected LR). Transformer L=12 (3427M, dashed red) now converges with the corrected LR (5×10−5, max_norm= 0.3), reaching val CE 5.396 at 10k steps, but plateaus after iter 8500 (best 5.378). Initial grad spike at iter 500 (norm= 109) delays convergence, leaving a gap of 1.03–1.23 nats versus HRM variants at 10k. HRM variants converge steadily to 4.16– 4.36 (4–10× fewer parameters).

Figure 1: EqualParam learning curves (val CE, 10k steps). All three HRM variants outperform both Transformer baselines. HRM N × T = 4 (T = 2), which previously exploded under a higher LR, now converges stably to 4.450 after applying scaled initialization, extended warmup, and K/M-proportional gradient clipping (max_norm= 0.5). The three HRM variants outperform Transformer L=4 by 0.11– 0.32 nats. Initial values (iter 0, ≈10.9–11.7) are off-chart.

Table 13: HPSearch: budget-matched LR/warmup grid search, 10k steps, seed=42. Best per model in bold. HRM NT=12 best (4.308) outperforms T-L4 best (4.543) by 0.235 nats under matched hyperparameter search.

Figure 3: HPSearch val CE curves (10k steps, seed=42). Left: HRM NT=12 best config (lr= 1.5×10−4, w= 1000) achieves 4.308 (thick teal). Right: T-L4 best config (lr= 2×10−4, w= 1000) achieves 4.543 (thick blue). At their respective best hyperparameters, HRM leads by 0.235 nats.

Figure 2: UniTF val CE curves. HRM NT=12 (1229M, green) converges steadily to 4.177 at 10k steps. All UniTF variants plateau at ≈7.6 regardless of width (820M or 1218M), learning rate, warmup, or initialization. v2b and v2c started from a bad random seed (iter-0 CE> 90); curves shown from iter 500 after recovery. The ≈3.4-nat gap at matched parameter count (1218M vs 1229M) is consistent with a structural rather than parameter-count advantage for HRM.
Table 22: M=48 results (10k steps, d = 4096, ≈1.23B params). M=12 reference values (10k, converged) shown for comparison.
Table 2: Key hyperparameters, derived quantities, and parameter sets.
